nativeRange.habitat: Small, shallow, slow-moving streams draining the Brazilian Shield; sandy or fine-silt substrate with dense marginal and aquatic vegetation; first-order tributaries in the upper Paraná River basin

decorPreferences: dense aquatic plants (Java moss, Amazon sword, Vallisneria)
leaf litter and driftwood for shelter and tannins
low to moderate flow from sponge or gentle HOB filter

diet.notes: Benthic forager; accepts high-quality sinking micro-pellets, crushed flake, and wafers formulated for bottom-dwellers. Supplement with small live or frozen foods: bloodworm, micro-worm, daphnia, and brine shrimp nauplii. Wild diet comprises worms, benthic crustaceans, aquatic insect larvae, and plant/organic detritus. Do not rely on this species to clean up leftovers from other fish.

goodWith: nano fish (celestial pearl danio, Norman’s lampeye)
dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — with caution around fry
avoidWith: aggressive or predatory fish that can swallow 3 cm prey
strong-current-producing powerheads unsuitable for slow-water species
fish requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with soft-acid parameters

summary: A diminutive armoured catfish from upper Paraná basin streams in Brazil, reaching only ~3 cm. Distinct from Corydoras — Aspidoras retains its own genus after the 2022–2024 Corydoradinae revisions. Peaceful, diurnal, and best kept in groups of six or more in well-planted soft-water nano tanks.
careNotes: Aspidoras lakoi is one of the smallest catfishes available in the hobby, rarely exceeding 3–3.5 cm. Although superficially similar to dwarf Corydoras, it belongs to the distinct genus Aspidoras (Ihering, 1907), characterised by the presence of a supraoccipital fontanel, and was unaffected by the 2022–2024 Hoplisoma/Corydoras revisions (Dias et al.) — it remains Aspidoras lakoi Miranda Ribeiro, 1949. The species is endemic to small, shallow streams draining the Brazilian Shield in the upper Paraná system. These headwater streams experience moderate seasonal temperature variation (down to ~15°C in austral winter at the source, up to ~26°C in summer at lower reaches), making A. lakoi more cool-tolerant than many tropical catfish; a range of 20–26°C suits it well in aquaria. Maintain soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.2, gH 2–12). A fine sand substrate at least 3 cm deep is essential to protect the barbels, which are easily eroded on coarse gravel and indicate declining water quality when shortened. Keep in groups of at least six — smaller groups show stress and reduced activity. Provide dense planting and leaf litter cover. Use gentle filtration (sponge filter or baffled HOB) as the species dislikes strong currents. Like all Callichthyidae, A. lakoi is highly sensitive to copper-based medications; use only copper-free treatments. The locking dorsal and pectoral fin spines can deliver a mildly venomous sting — handle with care using a soft net and avoid bare-hand contact. The species is facultative air-breathing and may visit the surface in low-oxygen conditions. Never add to a biologically immature tank; ammonia and nitrite must read zero.
breedingNotes: Breeding follows the typical Corydoradinae pattern. Condition a group with varied live and frozen foods. Spawning is best triggered in a separate 40–60 L tank with a sponge filter and 3–5 cm fine sand; perform a 30–40% water change with slightly cooler, well-oxygenated water to simulate seasonal rainfall. Adults scatter small adhesive eggs on plant leaves and fine-leaved vegetation; remove adults after spawning to prevent egg predation. Eggs hatch in approximately 3–4 days at 22–24°C. Free-swimming fry accept infusoria and commercial fry powder initially, transitioning to micro-worms and freshly hatched brine shrimp nauplii within the first week. Breeding is considered moderately challenging due to the species’ rarity in trade and sensitivity to water quality.

primary.name: FishBase — Aspidoras lakoi
primary.notes: Primary taxonomic and morphological reference. Confirmed: max length 4.0 cm SL (male/unsexed); family Callichthyidae, order Siluriformes; native range upper Paraná River basin, South America; freshwater benthopelagic habitat; facultative air-breathing; IUCN Least Concern. Temperature and pH not shown on FishBase page — derived from Aqualog (22–25°C, pH 5.0–6.0) and genus-level cross-references.

additional.name: Aqualog.de — Aspidoras lakoi species page
additional.notes: Provided adult size 3.5–4 cm, temperature 22–25°C, pH 5.0–6.0, tank minimum 60–100 cm, behaviour described as sociable, group, diurnal. pH floor of 5.0 noted but raised slightly in care entry to 6.0 as practical hobbyist minimum aligned with other Aspidoras sources.

additional.name: PlanetCatfish Cat-eLog — Aspidoras lakoi
additional.notes: Specialist catfish database; confirmed family, genus, native range (upper Paraná basin, Brazil), and aquarium husbandry parallels with other Aspidoras. Corydoradinae-standard care notes (fine sand, groups, gentle filtration, sponge filter) cross-referenced.

additional.name: SciELO Brazil — Ecology of upper Paraná stream inhabited by Aspidoras fuscoguttatus
additional.notes: Peer-reviewed ecology study on an upper Paraná first-order stream supporting a congener (A. fuscoguttatus). Documents temperature range 15°C (headwater winter) to 27°C (mouth summer), slightly acidic headwater pH, sandy substrate, and facultative air-breathing behaviour under hypoxia. Applied to A. lakoi as habitat analogue given shared drainage system and ecology.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Aspidoras
additional.notes: Confirmed genus-level taxonomy: distinct from Corydoras by presence of supraoccipital fontanel; established by Ihering 1907; endemic to small shallow Brazilian Shield streams; most species have restricted ranges. Cross-referenced species count and distribution overview.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; spawns on plant leaves following cool-water change trigger. Remove adults post-spawn. Eggs hatch in ~3–4 days; fry accept infusoria then micro-worms and BBS nauplii. Breeding is uncommon in the hobby due to rarity of the species in trade.
